bibliographic_data.title: The Robustness of LWPP and WPP, with an Application to Graph Reconstruction
bibliographic_data.abstract: We show that the counting class LWPP [FFK94] remains unchanged even if one allows a polynomial number of gap values rather than one. On the other hand, it is impossible to improve this from polynomially many gap values to a superpolynomial number of gap values by relativizable proof techniques. The first result implies that the Legitimate Deck Problem (from the study of graph reconstruction) is in LWPP if the weakened version of the Reconstruction Conjecture holds in which the number of nonisomorphic preimages is assumed merely to be polynomially bounded. This strengthens the 1992 result of K"obler, Sch"oning, and Tor\'an that the Legitimate Deck Problem is in LWPP if the Reconstruction Conjecture holds, and provides strengthened evidence that the Legitimate Deck Problem is not NP-hard.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper shows that LWPP and WPP are robust enough to remain unchanged when their single target gap is allowed to be expanded to a polynomial-sized list.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors prove the robustness of LWPP by showing that allowing a polynomial number of gap values does not change the class. They also show that for some relativized worlds, even two target values yield a richer class than one value does.
outcomes: LWPP and WPP remain unchanged when their single target gap is allowed to be expanded to a polynomial-sized list
The Legitimate Deck Problem (from the study of graph reconstruction) is in LWPP if the weakened version of the Reconstruction Conjecture holds
Provides strengthened evidence that the Legitimate Deck Problem is not NP-hard
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we explore the robustness of the counting classes LWPP and WPP. We show that even when allowing a polynomial number of gap values rather than one, these classes remain unchanged. This result has implications for the Legitimate Deck Problem in graph reconstruction, suggesting it is in LWPP under certain conditions. The work also highlights the limitations of relativizable proofs in extending this robustness to superpolynomial numbers of target gaps and provides insights into related counting classes like C=P.
